answer:
I remember when I really wished to get into this summer program for aspiring artists. I had poured my heart into my application, spending countless hours polishing my portfolio and writing what I thought was a compelling personal statement. When I found out I didn't get in, it felt like a punch to the gut. I was devastated, honestly. I mean, I had imagined myself there, surrounded by fellow creatives, honing my skills and learning from professionals. 

The emotions were all over the place—disappointment, frustration, even a bit of self-doubt crept in. I thought maybe my art wasn't good enough or that I hadn't portrayed myself well in the application. But after taking some time to reflect, I realized that this experience taught me a valuable lesson about resilience. 

I learned that sometimes, despite our best efforts, things just don't go our way. And that's okay. It's important to accept rejection and realize that it doesn't define my worth or potential. Instead of wallowing in disappointment, I decided to channel that energy into improving my art. I took online classes, joined local art groups, and sought feedback from peers and mentors. So, in a way, this setback became a stepping stone for me, pushing me to grow and become more passionate about my craft. Ultimately, I think it's about how we respond to these challenges that truly shapes our journey.
title: Tell me about something you really wished for but couldn't get. What emotions did you experience? What life lessons did this teach you?

answer:
Well, something that really made me laugh lately was a funny incident during my graduation ceremony. So, while we were all waiting for our names to be called, one of my friends decided to jokingly impersonate our principal. I mean, he nailed it! He mimicked the principal's mannerisms and voice so perfectly that it caught everyone off guard. We were all trying so hard to keep it together, but eventually, we burst out laughing. It was just one of those moments where the tension of the day flipped into something hilarious.

Afterwards, I told some of my classmates about it during lunch. They thought it was absolutely hilarious, especially when I described how our principal walked around with this serious face. One friend even suggested that maybe he should take up comedy as a side gig! It just lightened everyone's mood, especially after all the stress of exams and college applications. 

I think what made it funny was the unexpectedness of it all. You usually don't expect someone to break the seriousness of a graduation ceremony, you know? It kind of reminded us that even in big moments like these, it's important to let loose and enjoy ourselves. Honestly, I found myself reminiscing about that moment even days later, and it has definitely become a memorable part of my graduation experience.
title: Tell me about something that made you laugh lately. Why was it funny? Who else did you tell, and what did they think?

One place I really enjoy going to is the local park near my house. It's meaningful to me because it feels like a little escape from the hectic pace of everyday life, especially now that I'm busy with university applications and school commitments. You know, there's something about being surrounded by nature that just helps clear my mind. 

I often go there after a long day of studying, and it really helps me unwind. Just the other day, I sat on a bench by the lake, watching the ducks paddle by, and it reminded me of how important it is to take a break sometimes. Also, I often meet up with my friends there, which makes it even more special. We usually grab some snacks and just hang out, talking about everything—from our college dreams to our favorite movies. 

Going to the park kind of recharges my batteries, if you know what I mean. I think having that balance between work and relaxation is crucial, especially at this stage in our lives. It's like, when I'm there, I can breathe a little easier and gain perspective on what's really important. So, yeah, it's not just a park to me; it's a place where I find peace and connection. It really helps me stay grounded amid all the chaos.
title: Which place do you enjoy going to? What makes it meaningful? How does going there help you?

I think older people can learn a lot from younger generations today, especially when it comes to technology and social issues. You know, growing up with smartphones and social media has completely changed how we communicate and view the world. For example, I remember when I first got my phone; it opened up so many avenues for connecting with friends and sharing perspectives. I've noticed that older folks sometimes struggle with tech, and this can be a barrier in their day-to-day lives.

Also, I believe younger people tend to be more open-minded and accepting of diversity. For instance, issues like mental health and LGBTQ+ rights have become really prominent topics for my generation. I've seen firsthand how young activists are pushing for change and advocating for equality. Older generations can learn from this by understanding the importance of empathy and being more inclusive. It's kind of refreshing, you know?

In addition, the flexibility and adaptability of younger people in facing challenges, like climate change, is something valuable. We're growing up in a world that's changing rapidly, and I think older generations can benefit from our innovative approaches to problem-solving. So, in summary, I feel that older people can gain insights into using technology effectively, embracing diversity, and being adaptable by observing and engaging with younger people. I mean, there's so much that we can share with each other, right? It's all about learning together and fostering a sense of community.
title: What could older people learn from young people today? Why do you think this?

I think one of the best ways people can help the environment is by making conscious choices in their everyday lives. You know, it's all about those small, sustainable actions we can take that collectively add up to a larger impact. For instance, I remember when my family decided to start recycling more rigorously. At first, it seemed like a hassle, but it became this really empowering routine for us. We started composting kitchen scraps too, which not only reduced waste but also enriched our garden soil. 

Also, reducing single-use plastics has become super important. When I go to the store, I always bring my reusable bags, and I try to avoid products with excessive packaging. It just makes sense, right? But, on a larger scale, I think supporting local businesses that prioritize eco-friendly practices can make a big difference as well. For example, farmers' markets often offer organic produce, and knowing that I'm supporting sustainable farming feels great.

I find that educating ourselves and others about environmental issues is crucial too. Like, when I attended a workshop on climate change, it really opened my eyes to how interconnected everything is. So, in short, helping the environment is about making informed choices and encouraging those around us to do the same. Ultimately, I believe that if each person commits to small changes, we can create a ripple effect that leads to significant improvements. That's why I think it's the most important thing we can do right now.
title: How can people best help the environment? Why is this the most important thing to do?

I think a good leader is someone who truly inspires and motivates others. You know, it's not just about having authority or being in charge; it's more about how they connect with their team. For me, one of the most important skills is effective communication. A good leader should be able to articulate their vision clearly and listen to feedback from their team. I remember in my high school debate club, our captain was really great at this. He always encouraged everyone to share their ideas, which made us feel valued and more engaged in the discussions.

Another crucial skill is empathy. A leader should understand and relate to the feelings and perspectives of others. Like, when we faced challenges during group projects, our teachers who were empathetic really helped us navigate those tough situations. They would check in on us and offer support, which made a huge difference in our motivation and morale.

Additionally, adaptability is key. The best leaders are flexible and can adjust their strategies based on changing circumstances. I mean, I've seen this in various clubs I've been part of where the plans often had to change due to unexpected issues, like scheduling conflicts or resource limitations. Leaders who could pivot and find solutions tended to keep the team's spirit high.

So overall, I believe that a combination of communication, empathy, and adaptability makes someone a good leader. They really need to foster an environment where everyone feels included and motivated to contribute their best.
title: What makes someone a good leader? Which skills matter most and why?

answer:
Honestly, I think it’s a really good thing that learning a foreign language is required in some schools. From my own experience, studying Spanish throughout high school definitely broadened my perspective. You know, at first, I just saw it as another subject I had to get through, but over time, I realized that being able to communicate in another language isn’t just about the grammar or vocabulary—it’s about understanding other cultures and connecting with people from different backgrounds. For example, last year, my class did this project where we had to interview native Spanish speakers in our community. Honestly, it was kind of intimidating at first, but by the end, I felt way more confident, and I actually made a few friends I wouldn’t have met otherwise.

Also, learning a foreign language can be super practical. I mean, in today’s globalized world, so many jobs value bilingual employees, so having those skills can really open up opportunities down the road. Personally, I’m applying to universities now, and a lot of them mention how language skills are a plus, not just academically but also in terms of personal growth. Of course, I get that for some students, it might feel unnecessary or maybe even a little overwhelming, especially if they don’t plan to travel or work abroad. But even then, the cognitive benefits—like improved memory and problem-solving—are pretty significant, and there’s a sense of accomplishment when you realize you can hold a conversation in another language.

So overall, I’d say it's a positive thing because it helps us become more adaptable, culturally aware, and honestly, more well-rounded individuals.
title: Studying a foreign language is required in some schools. Overall, do you think this is a good thing or a bad thing? Explain your opinion.

answer:
Honestly, I think having a healthy diet has a lot of important benefits. For example, when I started paying more attention to what I eat, I noticed I have a lot more energy throughout the day, and I don’t get those mid-afternoon crashes as often as I used to. Eating nutritious foods can help improve your concentration and even your mood, you know? Plus, it’s crucial for preventing things like diabetes or heart problems later on. I mean, most people know that eating healthy is good for them, but still, it’s not always easy to stick to that kind of lifestyle.

The thing is, unhealthy food is kind of everywhere. It’s so accessible and, honestly, it often tastes amazing. Sometimes, especially after a long day at school or work, grabbing fast food just feels convenient. Also, I think some people don’t have much time or maybe the resources to cook healthier meals every day. There’s definitely a social aspect too; like, when I hang out with my friends, we often end up eating pizza or burgers because it’s quick and everyone likes it. So, even though we understand the risks, it's hard to resist sometimes.

But there are realistic ways to make your diet healthier without giving up everything you love. For me, I try to include more vegetables and whole grains in my meals. Swapping soda for water is a really simple adjustment. Also, cooking at home lets me control what goes into my food—I actually enjoy experimenting with new recipes. I think making small, sustainable changes is better than forcing yourself into extreme diets. Ultimately, finding a balance works best for me, and I feel so much better when I’m mindful about what I eat.
title: What are some of the benefits of a healthy diet? If healthy food is good for you, why do people eat unhealthy food anyway? What are some ways you could make your diet healthier?

answer:
Honestly, if I could design my perfect vacation trip, I’d probably choose to go somewhere that combines both adventure and relaxation, like the coast of Italy or maybe the south of France. I mean, I’ve always dreamed of exploring charming little towns, trying authentic food, and just soaking in the local culture. If I had to pick someone to go with, it would definitely be my best friend from high school—we’ve been talking about traveling together for years, and I think her easygoing vibe would make everything more fun.

First, we’d spend a few days wandering through historic streets, checking out art galleries and little cafés—you know, really getting a feel for the place. I’m kind of obsessed with history, so visiting ancient landmarks or museums would be amazing. Also, I’d want to try activities that are typical for locals, like cooking classes or maybe sailing along the coast. For example, learning to make traditional Italian pasta sounds both challenging and super rewarding. 

Of course, I’d want some time just to relax by the beach, maybe reading or listening to music, because I think vacations should also recharge you. Another thing that’s important to me is meeting new people from different backgrounds, so I’d probably join group tours or talk to locals whenever possible. Overall, I feel like this kind of trip would not only be unforgettable but also help me grow personally—I mean, stepping outside my comfort zone and experiencing a new culture is just invaluable.

answer:
Honestly, one historical figure I really admire is Malala Yousafzai. I mean, she’s not super ancient or anything, but her impact has been pretty profound, especially when you think about how young she was when she started advocating for girls’ education. What I find most inspiring about Malala is her extraordinary courage—the fact that she stood up to the Taliban and continued speaking out even after surviving an attack is just incredible. You know, most people would probably step back after experiencing something so traumatic, but she used her own struggle to highlight much bigger issues that affect millions of girls worldwide.

Also, I feel like her determination kind of shows that even if you’re just one person, you can still make a significant difference. For example, her work with the Malala Fund has already helped so many young women get access to education who probably wouldn’t have had that chance otherwise. It’s not just about being brave—it’s also about being resilient and having a vision for a better future.

From her story, I think we can learn the importance of standing up for what’s right, even when it feels risky or intimidating. There’s something really empowering about refusing to let fear control your actions. For me, as someone who’s about to start university, I hope I can bring some of that same spirit into my life, whether that's speaking out on campus or supporting causes I care about. In the end, Malala’s example shows that advocacy and perseverance can actually shape society, which is honestly something we all need to remember.

answer:
You know, when people talk about measuring success in life, there are honestly so many different perspectives, and it kind of depends on what someone values the most. For some people, success is all about financial stability or having an impressive career, you know, like climbing up the corporate ladder or starting their own business. Others might see success more in terms of relationships—like having close friends, a supportive family, or maybe even helping out in the community. Some folks think personal growth and happiness are what really matter, which I totally get, because sometimes external achievements don’t actually make you feel fulfilled.

For me, since I just graduated high school and I'm applying to universities in the U.S., I find that my definition of success has evolved a bit. Right now, I measure my success mostly by the goals I've set for myself and how much progress I'm making towards them. For example, getting accepted into my top-choice university would definitely feel like a huge achievement for me. But I also think it's important to recognize smaller wins, like improving my study habits or developing new skills, since these things really shape my future.

Actually, the way I see it, success isn’t just about reaching big milestones. It’s also about staying resilient and adapting when things get tough. Like, last year was pretty challenging, balancing exams and extracurriculars, but I learned a lot about time management and perseverance. So, for me, success is kind of a mix of achieving my ambitions and growing as a person along the way. In the end, I guess I measure success by how proud and satisfied I feel with what I've accomplished, both academically and personally.

answer:
Well, the strangest news story I heard recently was definitely that one about the guy in Florida who called 911 because he ran out of beer during the lockdown. I mean, can you believe that? The guy was apparently just sitting around drinking beer all day since he couldn't go anywhere. When he ran out, he actually called 911 and asked them to bring him more beer! The emergency dispatcher was like, "Uh, sir, that's not what 911 is for." But the guy just kept insisting they bring him more beer. 

I guess the story made the news because it's just so absurd that someone would think calling 911 for something so silly is okay. It really shows how the lockdowns have made some people go a bit crazy. I mean, I've been bored and frustrated, too, but I would never call 911 to ask for beer. That's just wild. 

The main thing I think people can learn from this story is that 911 is for real emergencies only. I know we've all been cooped up for weeks and getting a bit stir-crazy. But calling 911 to ask for beer or anything else that isn't a true emergency is just ridiculous and wrong. The dispatchers and emergency responders have enough to deal with responding to real crises without people prank-calling or making foolish requests. So, I hope this incident reminds everyone that 911 is sacred and needs to be used responsibly. Let's not waste the time of our amazing emergency workers, especially during such stressful times.

answer:
Well, if I were the leader of this country, the first thing I'd do is try to bring people together. I feel like we've gotten so divided, and there's just so much anger between folks on the left and right. I'd try to find common ground - focus on what unites us rather than what divides us, you know? 

I think this needs to change because all this division is tearing our country apart. We spend more time fighting each other than tackling the real problems we face. Things like the economy, health care, education - these should be the priorities. But instead, our leaders just point fingers while nothing gets done. We need to unite as one America.

To help people, I'd launch national service programs to get young folks working together on community projects - cleaning up parks, tutoring kids, and helping the elderly. When you work side by side, it's a lot harder to see someone as the enemy. And these projects would actually improve people's lives across the country. I'd also hold town halls and listening sessions in every state to hear people's concerns and try to find solutions. 

The bottom line is that we have more that brings us together than drives us apart as Americans. But our leaders have lost sight of that. I'd work hard to heal these divisions because we'll only prosper if we remember that we're all on the same team. It's time to come together and start getting things done again.

answer:
Honestly, when I think about my parents, one quality that really stands out is my mom’s resilience. She has this amazing ability to stay strong and positive, even when things get stressful or uncertain. For example, a couple of years ago, our family went through a tough financial situation, and while it was definitely overwhelming, she managed to keep us all grounded. I remember her saying things like, "We'll find a way," and instead of panicking, she focused on practical solutions. I think this resilience not only helped our family overcome challenges, but it also made her a better person—more empathetic and, you know, more willing to help others who might be struggling.

I’ve learned so much from her attitude toward adversity. One thing that sticks with me is how important it is to maintain perspective and not let setbacks define your outlook. Like, whenever I face something difficult, whether it's exam stress or, I mean, applying to universities, I tell myself to take a step back and focus on what I can control. Her approach taught me to be adaptable and optimistic, which I think is essential, especially now that I’m sort of entering adulthood. It’s not just about being tough; it’s also about being resourceful and compassionate.

So overall, my mom’s resilience has had a huge impact on the way I handle challenges in my own life, and I really admire her for that. I hope I can carry that quality forward, both at university and beyond.

answer:
I think one of the more recent developments that causes more problems than it solves is social media, particularly among younger people. I mean, on the surface, it seems amazing because it connects us with friends and family and allows us to share experiences instantly. But if we look deeper, it actually creates a ton of issues.

For example, I've noticed, like, how social media can lead to a lot of anxiety and pressure. People are constantly comparing themselves to these idealized versions of others' lives, which can make them feel inadequate or insecure. I remember scrolling through Instagram one night, and it just felt overwhelming seeing everyone's perfect photos. It kind of made me question my own worth, which isn't healthy at all. 

Also, social media sometimes fosters misinformation. You know, it spreads false news quicker than the truth, and that can influence public opinion in really harmful ways. I can't tell you how many times I've seen friends share something sensational without checking if it's true. This leads to misunderstandings and can even cause panic over unfounded issues, which is just crazy.

So, while social media definitely has its perks, I think the negative impacts often outweigh the positives, especially for young people trying to navigate their identities and find reliable information. It feels like we need to strike a better balance, you know, between enjoying the connectivity it offers and managing the emotional and societal challenges it brings.

answer:
I think people should definitely keep learning throughout their lives, and I can't stress enough how important it is. You know, the world is constantly changing, so staying curious and open to new knowledge really helps us adapt. For instance, I recently took up coding just because I thought it was cool. I mean, at first, it felt overwhelming, but now I understand so much more about technology and how things work behind the scenes. That experience showed me that learning doesn't just stop in school; it can be fun and rewarding at any age.

Also, lifelong learning can enhance our personal and professional lives. When you pursue new skills or interests, it opens doors for new opportunities. Like, when applying to universities, I noticed that many programs value students who have diverse experiences. It makes you stand out, right? Plus, engaging with different subjects can spark creativity and inspire you in ways you didn't expect.

But, on the flip side, some people might feel overwhelmed by the pressure to constantly learn. I get that. It's easy to think we need to excel at everything. However, I believe it's more about the journey than the destination. Learning can be about enjoying the process and discovering what truly interests you, rather than chasing perfection.

So, in conclusion, I really think that embracing lifelong learning is not just beneficial, but essential for personal growth and adaptability in our rapidly evolving world.

answer:
Honestly, when I think about the most important events in history, the invention of the internet really stands out. I mean, it’s kind of hard to imagine daily life without it now, especially for someone like me who's applying to universities in the U.S. The internet totally transformed how people communicate, access information, and even connect across cultures. For example, all the resources I’ve used to prepare for my university applications—from researching schools to practicing my English—were available online. If the internet had never been invented, my whole approach to education would be drastically different. Well, I’d probably have to rely mainly on books and maybe the local library, which, honestly, would limit my chances to discover new opportunities or learn about different perspectives.

Also, I think my worldview wouldn’t be as broad as it is now. I’ve been able to interact with people from all over the world through social media, and those experiences have made me a lot more open-minded and adaptable. You know, even the way we apply to universities today is so much more streamlined because of digital platforms—submitting applications electronically, accessing virtual campus tours, and attending webinars. Without the internet, that entire process would be way more complicated and probably less inclusive.

I guess what we can learn from this event is that technological innovation can really accelerate progress and break down barriers. But at the same time, it’s super important to use these advancements responsibly, since they come with challenges like misinformation or privacy concerns. So, the invention of the internet isn’t just about convenience—it’s totally reshaped our society and the way we connect with the world.

answer:
Well, I've always been fascinated by outer space and astronomy. Ever since I was a little kid, I loved looking up at the night sky and wondering what was out there. As I got older, I really wanted to understand more about the universe, like what planets are made of, how stars work, and if there's other life out there. 

I think the best way for me to learn more about space would be to take some classes at the local college. They offer a couple of intro astronomy courses that teach you about the solar system, stars, black holes, and stuff like that. I could also try joining their astronomy club to meet other people who are in space and do some stargazing events. Checking out some books from the library is another easy way to pick up more knowledge. 

If I learned more about astronomy and outer space, I think it would help me in life by satisfying my curiosity about the universe. I've always had this longing to understand what's out there beyond our planet. Learning more would make me feel more connected to the cosmos and our place in it. It could also inspire me in other areas of life, like realizing how small we are compared to the grand scale of the universe.

Overall, I think devoting some time to studying astronomy would enrich my life. I'd have a deeper appreciation for the night sky and our solar system. It's a big mysterious universe out there, so being able to learn about it is pretty cool. Satisfying my curiosity about space would give me more perspective and help me feel more in touch with the amazing wider universe we live in.

answer:
Honestly, I think working together is more important than competition, especially when it comes to learning and growing as a person. I mean, don’t get me wrong—competition does motivate people to push their limits and sometimes it’s kind of exciting. But in my experience, real progress happens when people cooperate, share ideas, and support each other. For example, in high school, most of the projects I did that actually felt rewarding were the ones where we had to collaborate as a team. Sometimes, someone would notice something I totally missed, or maybe I’d come up with a creative solution because of another person’s perspective. You know, being able to trust your teammates and build off each other's strengths is a much more sustainable way to succeed.

Of course, a bit of competition can be healthy—it pushes you to improve and gives you some ambition. But if everyone’s just competing all the time, it can create tension and make people reluctant to help each other out. Actually, I remember preparing for the SAT with some friends, and instead of competing for the highest score, we formed a study group. People shared resources and explained tricky concepts, and that made a huge difference in our confidence. I find that when you work together, you develop better communication skills and even empathy, which are super valuable in university and beyond.

So, while competition has its place, I honestly believe collaboration is way more beneficial in the long run. It not only helps you achieve your goals but also builds a sense of community that really matters, especially in an academic environment.

answer:
I had a really vivid dream recently where I was backpacking through Europe with some close friends from college. We had the most amazing time traveling together, taking trains between cities, staying in hostels, and just soaking up all the history, food, and culture. 

In the dream, we spent a few days in London, visiting all the famous sights like Big Ben, Buckingham Palace, and the London Eye. Then we took a train through the French countryside to Paris. I'll never forget how clearly I could imagine strolling through the streets of Paris and sitting in a cafe enjoying a croissant and espresso.

Next, we were off to explore Rome and all of its incredible ruins and architecture. We saw the Colosseum, the Roman Forum, everything. It was magical! The detail I could see in the dream was unbelievable, right down to the cobblestone streets and vibrantly painted buildings.

I think this dream represents my deep desire to travel through Europe with close friends. It's something I've always wanted to do but haven't made a reality yet. The dream was so vivid it made me seriously start planning to make the trip happen someday soon. I would absolutely love for this dream to come true. Exploring Europe would be such an inspiring, joyful adventure. Getting to experience new places and cultures with some of my best friends from college would be an absolute dream come true. We always said we'd travel together one day, and now it feels like the right time to finally make that happen.

So in summary, this wonderful dream was my subconscious telling me to pursue my travel goals now. It showed me how exciting and enriching the experience would be. I'm going to start putting a plan in place. Making this dream a reality will be one of the highlights of my life.

answer:
Honestly, if I could be any character from a film or book, I think I’d choose Hermione Granger from the Harry Potter series. I mean, she’s just such an inspiring character, you know? She’s incredibly intelligent, resourceful, and she always stands up for what she believes in, even when it’s difficult. What makes her really interesting to me is how she balances her academic ambition with genuine compassion for others. For example, she doesn’t just focus on getting good grades—she also cares deeply about her friends and fights for justice, like when she started S.P.E.W. to support house elves. 

I guess part of the reason I admire her so much is that I relate to her drive to achieve things academically, especially now that I’m applying to universities in the States. It can feel pretty overwhelming sometimes, but seeing how Hermione handles challenges makes me believe I could do it too—maybe with a bit more courage and initiative. If I were her, I think I would learn a lot about persevering through adversity, and also about not being afraid to stand out or challenge the status quo. 

Also, she never lets anyone’s opinion stop her from doing what’s right, which I find really impressive. I feel like stepping into her shoes would teach me resilience and how to advocate for others, not just myself. So, overall, I’d love to experience the kind of personal growth she goes through, and maybe develop some of those leadership qualities she has.

answer:
Honestly, if I had to pick one hobby or free-time activity that I'm most interested in, I’d definitely say photography. I mean, I've always been kind of drawn to visual arts, but photography, you know, is special for me because it allows me to capture moments and tell stories without words. I started getting into it about three years ago, mostly just taking pictures with my phone at first, but then I got a secondhand camera and, well, that really changed everything for me.

What I enjoy most is how photography sort of forces me to be present and notice details that I wouldn’t have paid attention to before—the way sunlight falls on a street, or how people interact naturally. For example, last summer I did a small project photographing local community events, and it made me realize how much diversity and energy there is in my town. Also, there's this creative aspect where I can experiment with composition and perspective, and honestly, I find it kind of liberating because there isn’t just one right way to do things.

In terms of personal development, photography has helped me become more observant and patient. Sometimes I’ll wait for hours just to catch the perfect shot, so I’ve learned to appreciate timing and persistence. It’s also helped me connect with others—like sharing photos online or discussing techniques with friends—which has definitely improved my communication skills. Overall, I think this hobby has made me more open-minded and reflective, and I’m sure these qualities will help me as I move on to university in the States.

answer:
Honestly, when I think about someone who inspires me, my grandmother immediately comes to mind. She’s just an incredible person—super resilient and always so optimistic, even when things got tough for our family. One of my favorite memories with her is probably those long afternoons we spent baking together during summer break. I remember one time when everything was just chaotic in the kitchen; flour everywhere, cookies burning—but she never lost her patience. Instead, she laughed it off and taught me how to improvise. That moment sticks with me, because it showed me her approach to life: you know, adaptability and staying positive even when things don’t go as planned.

I mean, what I admire most about her is how she’s faced challenges throughout her life. She grew up in pretty difficult circumstances, had to work hard for everything, but she always believed that persistence and kindness can get you through almost anything. For example, she would tell me stories about having to manage her small business while raising a family, and she never complained—she just found creative solutions.

From her life, I’ve learned that resilience and flexibility are essential, especially now that I’m preparing to transition to university in the US. I try to apply her lessons by embracing new situations and not letting setbacks discourage me. Also, I think cultivating optimism, like she does, really helps maintain balance when things feel overwhelming. So, overall, I’d say she’s taught me a lot about perseverance and having an open mindset, which are qualities I hope to carry forward.

answer:
Honestly, that's a pretty tough question, and I think my answer kind of depends on the situation. But if I had to choose, I’d say the group is probably more important overall, especially when we’re talking about things like communities or solving big issues. You know, as someone who just graduated high school and is hoping to study in the U.S., I’ve seen how powerful teamwork can be. For example, when my classmates and I worked together on a science project, our ideas sort of complemented each other, and we ended up coming up with a really innovative experiment. If I’d tried to do it all on my own, I’m sure I would have missed some essential perspectives.

But, well, at the same time, I don’t want to ignore the value of the individual, because every group is made up of unique people. I mean, sometimes a single person’s creativity or leadership can make a huge difference – like, one of my friends started a volunteering club at our school, and her initiative basically inspired others to get involved. So, individuals definitely matter, but I feel like the group amplifies what each person can contribute.

Also, in university settings, collaboration is crucial. You’re constantly engaging in discussions and group work, which really boosts your communication and critical thinking skills. Sure, the individual opinions matter, but collective action leads to meaningful change. Overall, I think both the group and the individual are essential, but in most cases, the group tends to have a bigger impact. That’s just how I see it.
title: What do you think is more important-the group or the individual? Provide reasons for why you think the way you do.

answer:
I think one thing every person needs in order to be happy is a sense of purpose. Having goals, dreams, or a passion to pursue gives life meaning and fulfillment beyond just getting by day-to-day. Without any larger sense of purpose, it's easy to feel lost or like you're just going through the motions. 

Personally, I've found that having creative outlets provides a huge sense of purpose. I love writing, making art, and playing music in my free time. Even though they're just hobbies for me, having those creative activities that I'm passionate about gives me something fulfilling to pour my energy into. On my toughest days, I can turn to my art or music to re-center myself. 

Having strong relationships also contributes to happiness and purpose. Whether it's family, friends, or a significant other, feeling connected to others on a deeper level provides comfort, understanding, and joy. Knowing there are people who unconditionally care about you gives you security and community.

Finally, finding meaning in helping others is huge. Volunteering, mentoring someone, or even small acts of kindness to brighten someone's day can provide a sense of purpose. When you make a positive impact on the world around you, it brings significance to your own life.

In the end, I think people need to find what gives their life meaning, whether it's creativity, relationships, helping others, or something else entirely. Discovering and pursuing your unique sense of purpose is crucial for long-term happiness and fulfillment.

answer:
One thing I can do now that I definitely could not do a year ago is speak in front of large groups. I used to get incredibly nervous any time I had to give a presentation in class. My hands would shake, my voice would quiver, and I just wanted it to be over as fast as possible. But over the past year, I've really pushed myself to get more comfortable with public speaking.

It started small - volunteering to read out loud in my English class or participate in class discussions. From there, I moved up to give short presentations in front of the class. Each time, I practiced over and over until I knew my material backwards and forwards. With each presentation, I gained a bit more confidence. 

My biggest test was when I tried out for the speech and debate team this year. I had to give a 5-minute persuasive speech in front of 50 students and teachers during the audition. A year ago, I could barely get through a 1-minute book report without stumbling over my words. But at the debate team tryouts, I spoke clearly and confidently the whole time. Though I was still nervous, I didn't let it paralyze me.

Pushing myself out of my comfort zone to speak in front of others has been hugely rewarding. I've proven to myself that I can overcome my fears and be an effective public speaker. My self-confidence has grown tremendously thanks to all the practice I've put in. Though I still get a little anxious before big speeches, I now have the tools and experience to handle it. I'm proud of how far I've come in one short year!

answer:
I think the most important thing I need to be happy in life is a sense of belonging. You know, it's that feeling of being connected to others and being part of a community that really makes life enjoyable. I mean, I've always found that when I'm surrounded by friends and family, I feel more fulfilled and at ease. For example, during my last summer, I volunteered at a local community center, and it was such an incredible experience. I got to know different people from diverse backgrounds, and we really bonded over shared activities like organizing events for kids. It felt amazing to contribute and see how we were all working towards something meaningful together.

Also, I believe this sense of belonging fosters emotional support. Whenever I face challenges or uncertainties, having people I can rely on—whether it's friends, family, or mentors—gives me the confidence to push through tough times. I remember when I was preparing for university applications; it was overwhelming, but having my friends to share that experience made it way more manageable. We would study together and encourage each other, which definitely eased the pressure.

Ultimately, I think happiness isn't just about personal achievements or material possessions, you know? It's really about those deep, interpersonal connections. So, for me, having a supportive community is absolutely essential. It not only brings joy but also security, allowing me to pursue my goals more passionately and with a greater sense of purpose.

answer:
Honestly, the last time I laughed out loud was just a couple of days ago when my friend and I were hanging out at this little café near my house. So, we were talking about all the weird things that happened to us during online classes, and she started telling this hilarious story about how her cat literally crashed her Zoom math lesson. You know, it’s already awkward enough having your camera on, but apparently her cat jumped onto her keyboard and accidentally turned on all these random filters—like, her face became a potato on screen, and nobody knew how to turn it off. I mean, I was just picturing our teacher trying to stay serious while explaining quadratic equations to a bunch of students, and there's my friend sitting there with a potato face. Every time I think about it, I can’t help but burst out laughing because the whole thing is so ridiculous.

Also, what made it extra funny was the reactions from the other students. Some people tried to give tech advice, but most of us were just losing it, especially when her cat walked through wearing one of those cones after the vet. It was like a comedy show happening in the middle of class. I guess I find moments like that really entertaining because they break up the monotony and remind us not to take everything too seriously. For me, that experience was genuinely spontaneous and, I don’t know, kind of relatable because everyone has had something silly happen in an online class. It’s those unexpected, absurd moments that make life feel a bit lighter.

answer:
One really basic skill that a lot of people struggle with is cooking - preparing healthy, tasty meals at home. Knowing how to cook is so important, yet many people don't have the knowledge or confidence in the kitchen.

In my opinion, the best way to teach cooking skills is through hands-on classes that get people comfortable with techniques and recipes. Show people how to chop, sauté, roast, and bake from scratch in a no-pressure environment. Let them eat what they cook. Make it fun - maybe throw in a glass of wine! Interactive demonstrations with an instructor guiding you will build cooking confidence.

Mastering some cooking basics would improve so many people's lives. You save money by eating at home versus grabbing takeout. You control what ingredients you use so you can eat healthier. Inviting friends over for home-cooked meals strengthens relationships. Knowing how to cook makes you more self-sufficient. And there's a real sense of achievement when you whip up tasty dishes yourself.

So many people resort to boxed and frozen meals because they think cooking is intimidating. But if we teach each other the basics - how to cook grains, prep veggies, use spices - in a hands-on, communal setting, more people would gain this life-changing skill. When you know how to cook, you tap into creativity, healthy eating, saving money, and deeper connections. Let's share the gift of cooking!

answer:
If I weren't afraid to fail, I would try starting my own small business. It's something I've dreamed about for a while, but the risk has held me back. 

I'd love to open a bike shop and cafe in my neighborhood. Cycling has been a huge part of my life since I was a kid, and I think it would be amazing to share my passion with the community. My shop would sell bikes, gear, and repairs while also serving coffee and hosting events. 

The reason this idea excites me is that it combines so many of my interests - cycling, coffee, connecting with people. I'd get to turn a personal passion into meaningful work. And I love the thought of creating a welcoming space for people to gather around a shared love of bikes.

Of course, launching a business like this would be a major financial gamble. Most new companies fail within a few years. I'd have to take out loans and invest my life savings. If things didn't work out, I could lose everything. But if I could get over that fear, I'd take the leap.

Owning a bike shop cafe has been my entrepreneurial dream for ages. If failure weren't a concern, I'd throw myself into making it a reality. Taking risks means occasionally falling on your face. But if I let fear hold me back, I'd always regret not trying. This business idea feels like exactly the type of fulfilling risk I should take.

answer:
I believe all young people should read Harper Lee's classic "To Kill a Mockingbird." Though published in 1960, its powerful themes of justice, empathy, and moral courage are just as relevant today.

This novel exposes the realities of racial injustice through the eyes of young Scout Finch in Depression-era Alabama. But more profoundly, it's about the formation of conscience in children, personified by Scout's father, Atticus Finch. Despite pressure to conform, Atticus exemplifies integrity in defending an innocent black man falsely accused of rape. 

Atticus models for his children critical thinking, sticking up for the vulnerable, and doing what's right even when it's unpopular. His steadfast morality in an immoral world teaches invaluable lessons about human decency. He instructs his kids to walk in others' shoes before judging them.

These themes of empathy, equality under the law, and strength of character are so important for young people to absorb. "To Kill a Mockingbird" teaches youth to think for themselves, challenge prejudices, and act with compassion in an unjust world.

Though set in a different era, the moral struggles it illuminates are universal. This book provides an insightful window into the childhood origins of conscience. It's essential reading for developing hearts, minds, and courage like Scout and Atticus Finch. What better guidance could we offer the next generation? That's why every young person deserves to read this modern classic.
title: What is a book that you believe every young person should be required to read? Explain the reasons for your choice.

answer:
The last time someone tried to tell me what to wear was for my buddy's wedding last summer. As a groomsman, the bride and groom requested I wear a specific suit for the ceremony. While I understood their vision for aesthetics, I felt a little frustrated having my outfit dictated.

On the day of putting on this assigned suit, I had no say in choosing; it made me feel like I was playing dress-up. It didn't reflect my personal style at all. I appreciated that they wanted uniformity, but it seemed overly controlling. I would've felt more like myself and comfortable in a suit I picked.

In my view, having autonomy over how we dress is hugely important for self-expression and confidence. The clothes we put on each day reflect our personality and mood. Fashion allows us to present ourselves to the world on our own terms.

When someone else mandates our clothing, it's disempowering. Being able to choose what makes you feel good is liberating. Of course, there are situations like work uniforms where rules may apply. But whenever possible, I think people should have the freedom to wear what makes them happy.

At the end of the day, it's just clothing. But the ability to self-determine your appearance has deep psychological value. It's about projecting your true identity, not someone else's imposed expectations. That choice and freedom matters.
title: When was the last time someone told you what to wear, and how did that make you feel? Is it important that people get to decide for themselves how to dress?

answer:
I would have to say that my hero from the past is definitely Nelson Mandela. I mean, he really inspires me because he stood up against oppression and fought for equality, not just in South Africa but around the world. His life story is incredible; he spent 27 years in prison for his beliefs, yet he emerged with such grace and a commitment to peace. That takes a kind of strength that I really admire.

What makes Mandela special to me is how he chose forgiveness over resentment. Instead of seeking revenge on those who wronged him, he aimed to unite his country. I remember reading about how he emphasized reconciliation, which is such a powerful lesson for all of us. It teaches that, no matter how challenging our circumstances may be, we can choose to approach life with hope and positivity.

His dedication also shows the importance of perseverance. You know, when things get tough, it's easy to give up, but Mandela's journey reminds me that real change often requires patience and resilience. It encourages me to look beyond my immediate struggles and think about the bigger picture.

So, I guess the main lesson we can learn from his life is that our actions matter. It's crucial to stand up for what we believe, even in difficult times. I find that incredibly motivating as I prepare for my own future and the challenges ahead in university and beyond. Mandela's legacy is a reminder that true heroes inspire change through their actions and ideals.
title: Who is your hero from the past? What makes them special to you? What lessons can their life teach us?

answer:
I remember a time when I had to give a speech in front of my entire school. Honestly, I was terrified. Public speaking has never been my strong suit, and the thought of standing on that stage made my heart race. But somehow, I decided to push through it. I think what made me brave was the support of my friends and teachers. They really believed in me, and I didn't want to let them down. So, I practiced relentlessly, rehearsing my speech until I felt more confident.

On the day of the speech, it was surreal. I could feel my palms sweating, but as I approached the podium, I took a deep breath and just focused on delivering my message. Surprisingly, once I started speaking, I found a rhythm. I shared my thoughts about the importance of environmental conservation, something I'm really passionate about. The more I talked, the more I felt connected to the audience. 

After that experience, I realized that facing your fears can be incredibly empowering. It taught me that growth often happens outside of our comfort zones. I've become more willing to take risks, whether it's applying to universities or engaging in discussions where I might have previously held back. And honestly, that day changed my perspective on challenges; now, I see them as opportunities for personal growth instead of obstacles. You know, I feel like I'm much more resilient now, and that's a lesson I'll carry with me moving forward.
title: Tell me about a time you did something that scared you. What made you brave enough to try? How has this experience changed you?

answer:
The best surprise I ever received was a trip to Hawaii that my wife planned for our 10th wedding anniversary. I had no idea she had been secretly saving up and planning this amazing vacation for months! On the morning of our anniversary, she surprised me with plane tickets, a hotel confirmation, and the whole package. I was completely shocked and overjoyed. 

I thanked her profusely, of course, and told her how meaningful this gift was - that she had put so much thought and effort into celebrating our relationship. While we were in Hawaii, I made sure to plan some special dinners and experiences to show my appreciation. And I still try to thank her for it even years later because it was such an incredible gesture of love. 

That trip brought us closer together and strengthened our marriage even more. We experienced a dream vacation together, made memories, and realized that after ten years, we still wanted to celebrate our relationship like it was new. It reminded me how lucky I am to be with someone who knows and cares about me so deeply.

So, in many ways, that surprise vacation was the ultimate gift. I will always remember and be grateful for the time and love my wife put into something she knew would thrill me. Knowing someone is willing to go the extra mile to create something unforgettable for you definitely made our connection deeper. I hope I can return to that level of thoughtfulness someday.

answer:
I think one thing that many teenagers do that is not good for them is spending too much time on social media. It seems like most teens these days are glued to their phones, scrolling through Instagram, Snapchat, TikTok - you name it. They spend hours a day just absorbing all this content. 

In my opinion, it's just not healthy. It creates this comparison culture where teens constantly see their peers' carefully curated lives and feel like they don't measure up. It exposes them to so much information and imagery that they're not equipped to process in a healthy way yet. And it can be addicting - the apps are designed to keep you hooked. Teens end up dependent on the constant dopamine hits from likes and comments.

I think the reason so many fall into this trap is that it's become the norm. All their friends are doing it too. They're afraid of missing out if they don't participate. Social media has just become ingrained into teenage social life. But I think if they stepped back, their mental health and self-esteem would improve. They'd be less anxious and stressed. They'd find more fulfillment in real-world relationships and activities. 

My advice for teens would be to set limits on social media use. Delete distracting apps off your phone if you need to. Find other hobbies that make you happy - sports, music, art. Spend more in-person time with family and friends. Your real life is so much richer than your online persona. Moderation and balance are key. You'll be so much better off in the long run.

answer:
The most beautiful place I've ever seen would have to be Banff National Park in Canada. I was there a few years ago on a road trip with my best friend, James. We had been camping and hiking through different parts of the park, and one day, we hiked up to the Lake Louise overlook. 

As soon as we crested the hill and Lake Louise came into view, I was absolutely speechless. The crystal blue water perfectly mirrored the surrounding snow-capped rocky peaks. It was like something from a postcard or paintings I'd seen of the Rockies. Except this incredible view was real and even more magical than I could've imagined!

I remember just standing in awe, taking it all in. James and I must've spent over an hour at the overlook, snapping photos from every angle. No picture could really capture the true majestic beauty before our eyes. The scale and natural perfection of those Canadian Rockies surrounding the glistening lake left me in total wonder.

Experiencing such an iconic, picturesque place in real life with my best friend is a memory I'll always cherish. The sheer beauty of it brought me so much joy and inspiration. It was a reminder of how precious and valuable it is to get out and enjoy nature's splendor firsthand. I hope I'm lucky enough to explore many more stunning places around the world. But Lake Louise set the bar incredibly high as the most wondrously beautiful spot I've set foot in so far.

answer:
I think the last big lesson I learned was actually about resilience, and it came from my history teacher. We were studying the Civil Rights Movement, and she shared personal stories about people who faced incredible adversity yet managed to persevere. You know, it really struck a chord with me because she emphasized how failure is often part of success, which is something I'd never really thought about before.

For example, she talked about Martin Luther King Jr. and how he encountered numerous setbacks while fighting for equality. It made me realize that achieving meaningful goals often involves overcoming obstacles, and I kind of found that inspiring. I mean, this lesson has been really relevant lately as I prepare for university applications. The process can be daunting, with so many rejections and uncertainties, but remembering that resilience is key makes me feel more equipped to face challenges head-on.

So, how will this help me later? Well, I believe that cultivating resilience now will serve me throughout my life. Whether it's academic pressures or personal struggles, I think I'll approach them with a stronger mindset, knowing that setbacks are just stepping stones rather than roadblocks. I remember feeling overwhelmed at times, but now I see those moments as opportunities to grow and learn. Ultimately, facing challenges with a proactive attitude will definitely help me not just in college but in any aspect of my future.

answer:
Well, when I think about kindness, one person who really stands out to me is my grandmother. She's always been this beacon of warmth and generosity in our family. For example, she volunteers at a local food bank every Saturday, packing meals for people who are in need. I mean, it's not just that she gives her time; it's the way she interacts with everyone around her that truly shows her kindness.

She's also taught me the importance of listening. Whenever someone shares their troubles with her, she really takes the time to hear them out, often offering a comforting word or two. It's a small gesture, but it makes such a big impact. You know, witnessing her approach to others has really influenced how I try to treat people in my life. 

I find that through her actions, I've learned that kindness isn't just about grand gestures but also about those simple, everyday moments. Like the time I was feeling overwhelmed with schoolwork, she made me tea and just sat with me, allowing me to vent. It was such a small thing, yet it meant the world to me.

So, I guess what I'm trying to say is that her constant demonstration of kindness has not only inspired me but also taught me how to be more empathetic and caring towards others. It's something I carry with me as I prepare for college and beyond, hoping to spread a little of that kindness wherever I go.
title: Who do you know that's really kind? What do they do that shows kindness? What have they taught you?

answer:
I don't think it's absolutely essential for a person to have friends who share the same language and culture. While it can provide comfort and familiarity, branching out can lead to valuable new perspectives and experiences.

On the one hand, friends from your own background understand subtle cultural references and traditions. You can communicate effortlessly without explaining contexts or translating sayings. A shared identity can help build an instant bond. 

However, only surrounding yourself with similar friends can become limiting. You miss opportunities to expand your worldview and learn from people different than you. Some of my most eye-opening friendships have been with international students or coworkers from diverse backgrounds. 

Gaining friends across cultures requires open-mindedness and effort. There are nuances to navigate, like different communication styles. But exposure to new outlooks has enriched my life. It's taught me to see issues from other angles and not assume my way is best. 

In an increasingly connected world, bridging language and cultural barriers is a valuable skill. Friendships across differences help us recognize shared hopes and struggles. While common ground makes bonding easy, reaching beyond your comfort zone fuels personal growth.

Ultimately, I think a well-rounded social circle includes familiar faces and new perspectives. Close friends who understand your roots provide reliability. But stepping outside your circle brings insight into how others live and think. Blending both similarities and differences keeps life vibrant, versatile, and full of learning.
title: Do you think it's important for a person to have friends who share the same language and culture? Why or why not?

answer:
One thing I tried that was really difficult for me was training for my first marathon. I'm not naturally a long-distance runner, so taking on 26.2 miles seemed daunting. There were many times I wanted to give up during the intense months of training. What kept me motivated was focusing on my goal to cross that finish line. 

I'd remind myself that every painful mile I ran was getting me closer to race day. Envisioning the pride I'd feel upon completing such a lofty challenge kept me going on tough training days. Having a training partner also made me accountable. Knowing someone was counting on me made it harder to slack off.

The experience taught me so much about self-discipline, resilience, and believing in myself. I discovered I'm capable of more than I thought I was - physically and mentally. It showed me the power of determination. When people doubted me, my persistence proved I could do hard things. Completing that first marathon taught me to embrace challenges. Now I know the satisfaction of pushing past perceived limits to achieve something great.

Though training was grueling, it increased my confidence and mental toughness enormously. I learned how to tune out discomfort and negativity. Focusing on the light at the end of the tunnel can get you through dark times. Running that marathon was one of my proudest moments. More than achieving a goal, it was a journey of self-discovery. I'll carry those lessons with me as I take on new challenges down the road.

answer:
I think my preferred way to travel between cities is definitely by train. It's just such a unique experience, you know? I love being able to relax and watch the scenery change as the train glides along the tracks. It feels so much more connected to the places I'm traveling through compared to flying, where you're basically in a metal tube in the sky. Plus, I mean, trains often have these spacious seats and you can move around, grab a snack at the café, or even just chat with fellow travelers.

For example, last summer, I took a train trip from Chicago to New York City. The ride took about 20 hours, but it was honestly one of the highlights of my whole summer. I sat by the window, and as we passed through different states, I got to see everything from farmland to urban landscapes. It felt like I was part of an adventure rather than just commuting. 

Another thing I really appreciate about trains is their environmental impact. They're generally more eco-friendly than cars or planes, which is something I think more people should consider when traveling. So, yeah, for me, it's all about the vibe and the journey itself, not just getting to the destination. Overall, traveling by train makes me feel more present and engaged with the world around me, which I absolutely love.

answer:
Well, I think people can definitely be dishonest without actually speaking. You know, it's not just about the words we say; sometimes, our actions or even our silence can convey a lot more than talking. For example, think about body language. A person might nod in agreement while actually disagreeing internally. Their gestures and facial expressions can completely contradict what they're saying, or in some cases, what they're not saying. It's kind of fascinating how that works.

Also, take social media as another example. People often portray an image that may not be accurate. They might post pictures or status updates that suggest everything is perfect, but in reality, they could be struggling. So, they're being dishonest, not through direct speech, but by crafting a misleading narrative about their lives. This sort of dishonesty can create unrealistic expectations for others, which is pretty concerning.

I mean, I've seen friends hide their true feelings or situations because they feel pressured to maintain a certain image. It makes you realize that honesty isn't always about verbal communication; it's about authenticity in all forms. In my view, this complexity adds layers to how we understand honesty and dishonesty today. So, while words are powerful, the unspoken elements—like actions, choices, and even silence—can be just as impactful, if not more. It's really important to consider these aspects because they shape our interactions and perceptions of each other.
title: Can people be dishonest without speaking? How might this happen?

4. **结尾**：  
   So, overall, I'd say that dishonesty can manifest in many forms beyond just spoken words. It’s really about how we convey our feelings and intentions through our actions. [Concluding Thought]."}

answer:
Winning a sports contest was honestly one of the most exhilarating experiences of my life. I remember when our basketball team made it to the finals last spring. You know, we had trained for months, and it really paid off in that crucial game. The atmosphere was electric, and I could feel the adrenaline coursing through me as we stepped onto the court. 

What contributed to our success, I think, was our teamwork. We each had our roles, and we trusted each other completely. During those final moments, when the score was tight, it was our ability to communicate and stay calm that made all the difference. I mean, there were definitely nerves, but we focused on execution rather than pressure. 

If I had to give advice to others looking to succeed in sports—or any competition, really—it would be to not only work hard but also foster good relationships with your teammates. Practice is important, but so is building trust and camaraderie. For example, after one particularly tough practice, we all went out for pizza and laughed about our struggles. That not only lifted our spirits but also strengthened our bond, which showed during the game. 

So, yeah, winning is fantastic, but it's really about the journey and the connections you make along the way. Embrace every moment! It sounds cliché, but it's true: you'll learn so much from both the victories and the defeats. Plus, those memories will stick with you long after the final whistle blows.
title: Tell me about winning a sports contest. How did you succeed? What advice would you give others?

answer:
I'm really excited to talk about the importance of studying abroad, especially for someone my age. You know, I think it's such a transformative experience that can really broaden your horizons. First of all, living in a different country exposes you to various cultures and perspectives, which is invaluable. I mean, when I think about it, just trying local foods or participating in festivals really helps you appreciate diversity in a whole new way.

For example, last summer, I went on a short exchange program to Spain. I was initially nervous about the language barrier and being away from home, but it turned out to be one of the best decisions I ever made. I quickly adapted and even made friends with locals. We explored the city together, and I learned so much about their lifestyle, traditions, and even history. It gave me a sense of independence that I never really had before.

Also, studying abroad opens up countless opportunities for academic growth. Universities often encourage students to take courses that might not be available at home, and you can gain unique insights and knowledge. I find that engaging with professors and peers from different backgrounds enhances critical thinking skills and adapts your worldview.

But, there are definitely challenges too, like homesickness or adjusting to a new education system. However, overcoming these obstacles really builds character. So, overall, I believe that studying abroad fosters personal and academic growth in ways that are truly unforgettable.

answer:
I remember a time when I saw something that really struck me as unfair. It happened during a basketball game in high school. One of my teammates, who was an amazing player, got treated differently by our coach just because he came from a less affluent background than some of the others on the team. You know, it was clear that he had talent, but it felt like the coach was favoring players who could afford all the latest gear and training. 

Seeing that made me realize how unfair circumstances can influence opportunities. I mean, it really frustrated me because talent should be what matters most, not someone's financial situation. I remember feeling angry, but I also felt motivated to do something about it. 

If I could fix it, I would advocate for a more inclusive approach in our team, maybe by discussing with the coach about recognizing skills over status. Also, I think setting up a fund or program within the school to help support athletes from lower-income families would be a good step. This way, no one has to feel sidelined because of their background. 

The lesson I learned is that fairness goes beyond just treating everyone equally; it's about ensuring that everyone has the same opportunities to shine, regardless of their circumstances. That experience definitely shaped how I view fairness and inclusion in sports and, honestly, in life too.

answer:
Honestly, I’ve thought about this issue quite a bit, especially now that I’m done with high school and getting ready for college in the U.S. Personally, I don’t agree with the idea of banning cell phones in schools entirely. I mean, on one hand, I get why some people think it’s necessary—phones can be distracting, and you know, sometimes students just end up scrolling through social media during class instead of paying attention. But actually, I feel that if we just prohibit phones altogether, we’re missing out on some real benefits.

For example, cell phones can be super helpful for research or collaborating with classmates. I remember in my senior year, my group used our phones to quickly look up academic articles and to coordinate projects. It made working together way more efficient. Also, having access to your phone is pretty important in emergencies. You never know when you’ll need to contact someone urgently, so restricting access seems kind of impractical.

On top of that, technology is such a huge part of modern education. Instead of banning phones, I think it makes more sense to teach students how to use them responsibly. Like, setting boundaries during lectures but allowing them for educational purposes. Well, obviously not everyone will follow the rules perfectly, but I find that fostering accountability works better than strict prohibition.

So, in my opinion, cell phones shouldn’t be completely banned in schools. With clear guidelines and trust, they can enhance learning rather than disrupt it. That seems way more realistic, especially as we prepare for university life and beyond.
title: Some people believe that cell phones should not be allowed in schools. Do you agree with this idea? Why or why not? Use specific reasons or examples to support your opinion.

answer:
Honestly, I think high school students should definitely have the opportunity to choose which subjects they study, at least to some extent. I mean, when I was in high school, I found that I was pretty passionate about literature and psychology, but not so much about physics. Having the flexibility to focus more on areas that genuinely interest you kinda motivates you to learn and actually enjoy school, you know? It’s a lot easier to excel when you’re engaged with the material, and I feel like letting students pick their subjects helps them discover what they might want to pursue in the future.

But at the same time, I do believe there are certain subjects everyone should study, just so we all have a basic foundation. For example, I think math and science are essential, not just because they're academically important, but also because they teach critical thinking and problem-solving skills. Also, English or language arts is crucial, since clear communication is key in almost any career. Honestly, history is another one I’d include, because it gives you perspective on current events and helps you become a more well-rounded person.

I guess what I’m saying is, while personal choice is super valuable, having some mandatory core subjects kind of ensures that students don’t miss out on fundamental knowledge. In my experience, the balance between required classes and electives worked pretty well—like, I could take advanced biology because I was interested, but I still had to study basic math and history. So, yeah, giving students autonomy in their education is important, but combining that with essential subjects seems like the best approach for preparing us for university and life in general.
title: Do you think that high school students should be able to choose which subjects they want to study? Why or why not? Are there particular subjects that you think all high students should study, and if so, what are they? Use reasons to support your opinion.

answer:
One electronic that I absolutely use every single day is my smartphone. I think most people today would say the same. I use my phone constantly to text friends, check social media, look up information online, listen to music or podcasts, and, of course, make phone calls. It keeps me connected, informed, and entertained at all times. I honestly can't imagine life without immediate access to everything my phone provides. 

I use my smartphone so much because it's just become an integrated part of my daily routine, from waking up and checking notifications to having it out on my desk at work to scanning social media during free moments to reading emails or articles during my commute, to video chatting with relatives in the evening. It's constantly by my side as both a tool and a source of distraction or leisure. I sometimes use it mindlessly just out of habit.

If I gave up my phone for a week, my life would definitely feel very inconvenient and disconnected. I'd lose my main source of communication, information, and entertainment. I'd have to revert back to old-school maps for navigation or actual books for reading material. It would force me to be more present in the moment instead of always looking at a screen. I imagine it would be frustrating at first, but ultimately healthy to take a break from the constant stimulation. I'd emerge from the week more appreciative of the incredible capabilities our smartphones provide us today.

So, in short, like most people, I'm completely reliant on my smartphone and all its functions in my daily life. It would be an adjustment to go without it, even temporarily, but could be a useful reset. Moderation in phone use is probably wise for all of us.

answer:
I remember the last time someone really helped me learn something—it was just this past summer when I was preparing for my university entrance exams. My older brother, who's already in college, noticed I was struggling with some of the math concepts. He sat down with me and went through everything step by step. You know, he didn't just give me the answers; he really took the time to explain the reasoning behind each problem. That made a huge difference because, honestly, I often get lost in the formulas. 

What struck me the most was how he related the math problems to real-life situations. For instance, he used examples from our daily lives—like budgeting for groceries—to make the concepts more tangible. It made it so much easier to grasp not just the 'how' but also the 'why' of the math we were working on. This experience taught me the importance of making learning relevant and relatable, which I think is key in helping others.

Now, I'm thinking about how I can apply this approach in the future, especially when I tutor younger students in subjects like math or science. I mean, if I can connect complex ideas to everyday scenarios, it might just bridge that gap for them too. Overall, I realized that patience, creativity, and personal connection are essential tools in teaching, and I hope to use what I've learned to make a positive impact, just like my brother did for me.
title: When was the last time someone helped you learn something? How did their help make it easier to understand? How could you use this experience to help others?

I would absolutely love to study in Japan. I think it's such a fascinating place, and the culture is incredibly rich, which makes it ideal for learning, you know? The blend of traditional customs and cutting-edge technology really captures my imagination. 

Studying in Japan would immerse me in a unique environment where I would not only learn about language but also about different ways of thinking. For example, the Japanese approach to education emphasizes discipline and respect, which I believe could enhance my learning experience. Plus, I'm really interested in exploring the local history and art scenes, like visiting ancient temples in Kyoto or experiencing the vibrant life in Tokyo. 

I want to achieve fluency in Japanese while also understanding the nuances of the language. I mean, communication is so much more than just vocabulary; it's about grasping cultural contexts too. Additionally, engaging with local students would give me insights into their perspectives, which could be beneficial for my personal growth and academic aspirations. 

So, overall, I see Japan as a place where I can combine my passion for language learning with cultural exploration. It's not just about hitting the books, but engaging with the world and broadening my horizons—this is why I think Japan would be the perfect place for me to study.
